video_title: How To Trade The New Warsh Fed | Bob Sheehan
gist: Bob Sheehan of Lighthouse Macro argues the 'Fed put' is dead under new Fed chair Kevin Warsh, who has slashed forward-guidance language and is reshaping both rate policy and the balance sheet. He frames the curve as two separate trades — a near-term bear flattener (short end higher) and a longer-term term-premium-driven long-end selloff — and positions defensively (healthcare, staples, short-duration equities) for a less-signaled, higher-volatility regime.

themes.theme: The Death of the Fed Put

insights.claim: The decade-long 'Fed put' — the market's assumption that the Fed steps in to backstop risk assets after hard sell-offs — has been meaningfully removed under new chair Kevin Warsh, who has signaled it is not coming back.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Investors can no longer reflexively buy dips expecting rate cuts and liquidity to floor prices; the historical backstop priced into risk assets is gone.
insights.quote: The Fed put is essentially the market's assumption that if risk assets fall hard enough, the Fed's going to step in

insights.claim: Even if the Fed put is not fully gone, the degree to which Warsh has removed it is large enough to change how investors should perceive Fed policy and price meetings and cuts.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: A partial removal of the put still justifies repricing risk premia and reducing reliance on dovish reaction functions.
insights.quote: even if I'm not fully right that it's fully gone, I think the degree that he's removed it is meaningful

insights.claim: With the Fed less direct about its intentions, the correct response is to be quicker to cut risk and avoid leaning heavily on any single bet, favoring a more defensive posture.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Reduced policy clarity is itself a reason to de-risk and size positions smaller, independent of the macro direction.
insights.quote: I'm going to be a little bit quicker to cut risk or I'm going to maybe not not overly lean on one bet hitting

insights.claim: The Fed's written guidance language was cut sharply (Sheehan cites roughly 340 words down to ~170, and statements now around 120-150 words), with Warsh abstaining from the dot plot, removing the 'bumpers' that previously guided market expectations.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Less explicit guidance means a wider range of outcomes per meeting and elevated rate volatility to trade around.
insights.quote: the words got cut from like 340 to 170

insights.claim: Removing forward guidance opens the door to a wider range of outcomes for meetings and dot plots, increasing realized volatility as 'talking heads' interpretations replace the Fed walking markets to a conclusion.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Vol sellers and consensus-trade crowders are more exposed; being correctly positioned against consensus can pay off big amid larger swings.

insights.claim: Nine of 18 FOMC officials shifted up their rate-hike expectations for September and December, reflecting greater internal disagreement that translates into market volatility.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Greater dispersion in the committee's views signals a less anchored policy path and supports a more two-sided rates market.
insights.quote: the jump to uh nine out of the 18 officials, right? Uh jumped up in terms of the hikes in September and December

insights.claim: Parsing the Fed's now-shorter ~120-word statements becomes more important precisely because there is less text to extract signal from, raising the value of careful language analysis.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Edge shifts toward those who can extract signal from sparse communication rather than relying on rich guidance.

insights.claim: Because the Fed will no longer talk markets through its thinking, Sheehan is shifting to evaluate economic releases (CPI, PCE, NFP) purely on the numbers and historical market reactions, putting less weight on how the Fed might interpret them.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Trade the data against its own historical reaction function rather than guessing the Fed's response — at least early in Warsh's tenure.
insights.quote: you should put less reliance on that as you're determining the data

insights.claim: Sheehan's macro process is explicitly probabilistic and falsifiable: he attaches historical base rates to views and predefines the data thresholds that would prove him wrong, rather than relying on headline-driven intuition.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: A disciplined, data-anchored framework with pre-set falsification points is more reliable signal than narrative-only macro calls.
insights.quote: here's where I could be definitely wrong

themes.theme: Two Curve Trades: Bear Flattener Then Long-End Steepener

insights.claim: The yield curve is now two distinct trades with two timings: a near-term bear flattener (short end moving higher faster than the long end) followed over months-to-quarters by a long-end selloff driven by supply and term premium.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Sequence the rates book: position for short-end-led flattening now, then rotate to a long-end steepener as supply pressure builds.

insights.claim: The short end is currently the focus and is moving higher quickly (a bear flattener), reacting to recent inflation prints, while the long end will be a slower trade over the coming month-plus.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Near-term rates action is concentrated at the front end; the long end lags before its own move materializes.

insights.claim: The long end answers to supply and a returning term premium rather than to the Fed, so it will be pushed higher over months by Treasury issuance and balance-sheet reduction independent of the hawkish-vs-inflation debate.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Long-end positioning should key off supply/term-premium dynamics, not just inflation expectations — a potential mispriced trade.
insights.quote: I think the long end really answers to supply a and the short end is it's still obviously it's always going to answer to the Fed

insights.claim: Foreign buyers of US Treasuries are broadly dropping off versus history, reinforcing the long-end supply problem despite some analyses arguing demand is unchanged.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Fading foreign demand is a structural tailwind for higher long-end yields and steeper curves over time.
insights.quote: mostly foreign buyers are dropping off compared to what they have been

themes.theme: Defensive Equity Positioning for a Muddied Regime

insights.claim: Sheehan is positioned defensively in healthcare, staples and short-duration equities, which are benefiting as the curve and policy regime stay 'muddied' and less signaled.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Rotate toward short-duration defensives over long-duration tech while the rate and policy path is unclear.
insights.quote: we are currently positioned that uh you want to be more defensive in this time while things are trying to kind of uh find their level

insights.claim: The equity reaction is fundamentally a duration story: long-duration tech names are most exposed to higher long-end yields, while value/defensive names are shorter-duration and more resilient.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Frame equity rotation through bond-like duration exposure, not just the AI narrative, when rates rise.
insights.quote: value defensives are shorter duration equities, longerterm tech names are going to be your longer duration equities

insights.claim: Both Bitcoin and gold are selling off together (gold down ~3% in a day), an unusual risk dislocation that breaks historical correlations and signals a non-standard regime.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Simultaneous gold and Bitcoin weakness flags that historical hedging relationships are breaking down and risk is being repriced broadly.
insights.quote: you're getting kind of both bitcoin and gold selling off. I think gold sold off like 3% the other day

themes.theme: Balance Sheet, Fiscal Doom Loop, and the Relative Game

insights.claim: Balance-sheet dynamics — reserve management purchases, bank-treasury deregulation, and Warsh's stated desire to shrink the balance sheet — are a real change in money supply that must be analyzed separately from rate policy, not lumped into a single Fed story.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Treat balance-sheet/liquidity mechanics as a distinct driver of the long end alongside rates and fiscal supply.
insights.quote: that's not words right that is an actual change in in the money supply

insights.claim: Warsh is building 'deniability' via task forces around balance-sheet decisions, using them as cover to launder conclusions he has already reached without committing publicly.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Read Warsh's process-heavy task forces as a signal of predetermined policy direction rather than genuine open deliberation.
insights.quote: building this deniability and this this kind of this task force around that are are kind of cover and some degree to maybe launder like the conclusions that he's come to

insights.claim: The US is in a 'fiscal doom loop' where war and government spending exceed lagging tax receipts, the highest-ever interest expense on the debt forces more issuance, and that issuance feeds further deficits and long-end supply pressure.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: The self-reinforcing deficit-issuance loop structurally pressures long-end yields higher and is underappreciated by markets.
insights.quote: we're paying more than we're getting in from tax receipts and and it becomes this loop

insights.claim: Sheehan rejects the doom-loop maximalist view that the dollar falls to zero; treasuries will still be bought and macro outcomes are fundamentally relative, judged against rivals like China rather than against an absolute.
insights.speaker: Bob Sheehan
insights.so_what: Avoid apocalyptic dollar-collapse trades; frame currency, oil and debt-sustainability views in relative terms versus alternatives.
insights.quote: the the dollar is going to fall to zero because of this fiscal and the only way to do it is to value like I don't think that's a realistic way to view the world

insights.claim: Since 2021, fiscal policy has become a far more dominant macro variable, compounding monetary and balance-sheet drivers and making the overall macro picture harder to forecast.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Fiscal flows now rival monetary policy as a market driver and must be weighted heavily in any macro view.
